nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral reefs across the Indo-Pacific, from clear coastal slopes to outer reef walls with moderate current. Unlike most blennies, the Midas Blenny spends much of its time hovering and swimming in open water, with individuals reported at 2–3 m above the substrate while feeding on zooplankton drifting in the current.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices for retreat

diet.notes: Atypical for the genus: in the wild it feeds primarily on zooplankton rather than algae. Generally advised to offer a varied meaty diet (mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped crustacean flesh) supplemented with frozen herbivore preparations and marine micro-algae. Best fed multiple small meals per day to support a high-metabolism planktivore.

goodWith: anthias (Pseudanthias spp.) — naturally schools with and mimics them
avoidWith: very small dartfish and chromis in modest tanks

imageCandidates.notes: By NasserHalaweh, 1692x1269. Full-body golden Midas Blenny with its diagnostic lyretail caudal fin, hovering in open water among lyretail anthias - directly illustrates the anthias-mimicry/shoaling behaviour that defines this species. Mild reef water haze but the foreground fish is in focus.

By Jason Marks, 2150x1507. Tack-sharp frontal portrait showing the blunt blenny head, cirri and bright blue eye-rings on a golden-olive individual. Face-on crop (body not fully in frame), so kept as a non-recommended detail shot.

summary: The Midas Blenny is a golden, planktivorous reef fish that hovers in open water rather than grazing rock like most blennies. It is well known for schooling alongside lyretail anthias and mimicking their coloration, a behavior that often continues in the aquarium.
careNotes:
The Midas Blenny is an unusual member of the genus Ecsenius. Unlike most combtooth blennies, which perch on rock and graze algae, it spends most of its time hovering and swimming in open water 2–3 m above the reef, feeding on zooplankton. In nature it is well known for joining schools of lyretail anthias (Pseudanthias squamipinnis) and other anthias species (Bartlett's, Dispar, Whitley's, Evans') and mimicking their coloration and swimming pattern — this behavior often carries over to the aquarium, where a Midas Blenny tends to shoal with a group of anthias rather than retreating into the rockwork. The species also has a documented color-change ability, with an orange-yellow phase associated with anthias-mimicry.

Despite a peaceful reputation in retail care sheets, the Midas Blenny is generally regarded as more aggressive than other Ecsenius blennies. Conspecific aggression is high: keeping more than one Midas Blenny together is generally discouraged unless the tank is very large or a confirmed pair is obtained. It will also frequently quarrel with other Ecsenius species and has been known to nip at and harass small planktivores such as firefish, dartfish, and small gobies, particularly in cramped tanks. A species-only tank is sometimes suggested for this reason, though it is most commonly kept singly alongside larger, peaceful reef fish. Larger tanks tend to reduce its aggressive tendencies considerably.

The Midas Blenny is generally considered reef-safe and does not nip at corals or sessile invertebrates. As a scaleless fish it is sensitive to water-quality swings and copper-based medications, so a stable, well-matured reef is advised. Like most blennies it is an accomplished jumper and a tightly fitted lid is strongly recommended.
breedingNotes: The Midas Blenny is an egg-laying species that forms distinct pairs during the breeding season. Eggs are demersal and adhesive, anchored to the substrate via a filamentous pad, while the larvae that hatch from them are planktonic and require microscopic live foods. Captive breeding of Ecsenius midas is rarely reported, and no established home-aquarium breeding protocol is widely recognised.

additional.notes: Source for anthias-mimicry behavior, planktivorous diet (atypical for genus), and 6+ year aquarium lifespan for Ecsenius blennies.
